context:
Section 7 (Concluding comments), page 29 (paper pagination shown as 28 in the provided text dump), under the heading 'two open questions', item 1

With lower bounds it is not clear what the definition of competitiveness should be: how to account for the situation where a lower bound \(\underline s_a\) forces the agents to populate a post \(a\) that they unanimously loath?
title: Define and justify competitive fairness under lower congestion bounds setting
background_latex:
Let there be a finite set of posts (items) \(A\) with \(|A|=m\) and a finite set of agents \(N\) with \(|N|=n\). A (deterministic) assignment is a family \(P=(S_a)_{a\in A}\) of pairwise disjoint subsets of \(N\) whose union is \(N\), where \(S_a\) is the set of agents assigned to post \(a\). Under anonymous congestion, the congestion at post \(a\) in assignment \(P\) is \(s_a:=|S_a|\), and agent \(i\)'s realized allocation is the pair \((a,s_a)\) when \(i\in S_a\). Each agent \(i\in N\) has an ordinal preference relation \(\preceq_i\) over all feasible allocations \(A\times \{1,2,\dots,n\}\), assumed to be strictly decreasing in the congestion coordinate at each fixed post (i.e., for each \(a\), \((a,s+1)\prec_i (a,s)\) for all \(s\)).

Now introduce post-specific congestion bounds: for each \(a\in A\), a lower bound \(\underline s_a\in\{0,1,\dots,n\}\) and an upper bound \(\overline s_a\in\{0,1,\dots,n\}\) with \(\underline s_a\le \overline s_a\). Feasible assignments are those with \(\underline s_a\le s_a\le \overline s_a\) for all \(a\in A\), and \(\sum_{a\in A} s_a=n\). In a setting with bounds, a central fairness notion of the paper (without bounds) is a competitive assignment: an assignment \(P\) with congestion profile \(s\) is competitive if for every agent \(i\in S_a\) and every post \(x\in A\), agent \(i\) weakly prefers \((a,s_a)\) to the hypothetical move \((x,\max\{s_x,1\})\) (interpreting an empty post as having a minimal effective congestion of 1).

With lower bounds \(\underline s_a>0\), some posts must be populated even if all agents dislike them, creating tension with the usual interpretation of competitiveness (envy-freeness-like conditions using congestion as a price).
problem_definition_latex:
Give a mathematically well-defined notion of \emph{competitiveness} (a competitive-fairness concept) for congested assignment problems with anonymous congestion and post-specific lower bounds \((\underline s_a)_{a\in A}\) and upper bounds \((\overline s_a)_{a\in A}\) on the congestion levels, and provide a principled justification that this notion appropriately accounts for forced population of posts when \(\underline s_a>0\), i.e., when some posts must host at least \(\underline s_a\) agents even if agents would prefer to avoid them.

In particular, specify how the competitive comparison \((a,s_a)\) versus \((x,\cdot)\) should be defined when feasibility requires \(s_x\ge \underline s_x\) for all \(x\), and address how to treat situations where a lower bound forces assignment to a unanimously disliked post.
latex:
Let there be a finite set of posts (items) \(A\) with \(|A|=m\) and a finite set of agents \(N\) with \(|N|=n\). A (deterministic) assignment is a family \(P=(S_a)_{a\in A}\) of pairwise disjoint subsets of \(N\) whose union is \(N\), where \(S_a\) is the set of agents assigned to post \(a\). Under anonymous congestion, the congestion at post \(a\) in assignment \(P\) is \(s_a:=|S_a|\), and agent \(i\)'s realized allocation is the pair \((a,s_a)\) when \(i\in S_a\). Each agent \(i\in N\) has an ordinal preference relation \(\preceq_i\) over all feasible allocations \(A\times \{1,2,\dots,n\}\), assumed to be strictly decreasing in the congestion coordinate at each fixed post (i.e., for each \(a\), \((a,s+1)\prec_i (a,s)\) for all \(s\)).

Now introduce post-specific congestion bounds: for each \(a\in A\), a lower bound \(\underline s_a\in\{0,1,\dots,n\}\) and an upper bound \(\overline s_a\in\{0,1,\dots,n\}\) with \(\underline s_a\le \overline s_a\). Feasible assignments are those with \(\underline s_a\le s_a\le \overline s_a\) for all \(a\in A\), and \(\sum_{a\in A} s_a=n\). In a setting with bounds, a central fairness notion of the paper (without bounds) is a competitive assignment: an assignment \(P\) with congestion profile \(s\) is competitive if for every agent \(i\in S_a\) and every post \(x\in A\), agent \(i\) weakly prefers \((a,s_a)\) to the hypothetical move \((x,\max\{s_x,1\})\) (interpreting an empty post as having a minimal effective congestion of 1).

With lower bounds \(\underline s_a>0\), some posts must be populated even if all agents dislike them, creating tension with the usual interpretation of competitiveness (envy-freeness-like conditions using congestion as a price).

Give a mathematically well-defined notion of \emph{competitiveness} (a competitive-fairness concept) for congested assignment problems with anonymous congestion and post-specific lower bounds \((\underline s_a)_{a\in A}\) and upper bounds \((\overline s_a)_{a\in A}\) on the congestion levels, and provide a principled justification that this notion appropriately accounts for forced population of posts when \(\underline s_a>0\), i.e., when some posts must host at least \(\underline s_a\) agents even if agents would prefer to avoid them.

In particular, specify how the competitive comparison \((a,s_a)\) versus \((x,\cdot)\) should be defined when feasibility requires \(s_x\ge \underline s_x\) for all \(x\), and address how to treat situations where a lower bound forces assignment to a unanimously disliked post.
location_in_paper: Section 7 (Concluding comments), page 29 (paper pagination shown as 28 in the provided text dump), under the heading 'two open questions', item 1
open_problem_quote: With lower bounds it is not clear what the definition of competitiveness should be: how to account for the situation where a lower bound \(\underline s_a\) forces the agents to populate a post \(a\) that they unanimously loath?
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question asks for an appropriate definition and normative justification rather than a universal claim that could be falsified by a finite instance. While examples can illustrate failures of candidate definitions, there is no clear finite search space whose automated exploration would resolve the definitional issue.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: An evolutionary loop could enumerate candidate competitiveness definitions (e.g., variants of envy-freeness with feasibility-aware deviations) and test them computationally on randomly generated bounded-congestion instances against desiderata such as existence, uniqueness, and efficiency. However, the core task is conceptual and axiomatic, so a computable fitness proxy would only partially capture the required normative justification.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The forward-citation evidence provided contains only one citing work, which develops and analyzes the competitiveness concept for congested assignments without post-specific lower bounds. In that baseline model, competitiveness is defined by a no-envy/no-profitable-virtual-move condition comparing an agent’s realized allocation \((a,s_a)\) to \((x,\max\{s_x,1\})\), thereby assigning an effective congestion of 1 to empty posts. This device is central to ruling out degenerate envy-free outcomes where everyone piles onto a single post while leaving others empty.

However, once feasibility imposes lower bounds \(\underline s_a>0\), the interpretation of \(\max\{s_x,1\}\) breaks: posts cannot be left empty, and agents may be forced into unanimously disliked posts to meet \(\underline s_a\). The citing paper explicitly flags this as an open question and does not propose a replacement competitive comparison or equilibrium-like condition that internalizes the mandatory minimum occupancies. Therefore, based on the provided forward-citation analysis, the problem of defining and justifying competitive fairness under lower congestion bounds remains open.

sources.title: Assignments for Congestion-Averse Agents: Seeking Competitive and Envy-Free Solutions
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper defines deterministic competitiveness for anonymous congestion as: an assignment 
- is competitive iff for all posts 
- for all agents 
- 

It proves that if a competitive assignment exists then it is essentially unique in congestion and welfare, efficient (under mild conditions), and implies the paper’s ex ante guarantee (top-\(n\)-fairness): see Proposition 3. It also proves that with vNM utilities there always exists a unique competitive fractional congestion profile \(\sigma^c\) (up to posts with \(\sigma_a^c\le 1\)), implementable by a lottery over deterministic assignments that round \(\sigma^c\) up/down and are approximately competitive/fair/efficient: Lemmas 1–2 and Theorem 1.
sources.connection: The open problem asks to define and justify a notion of competitive fairness when posts have lower bounds \(\underline s_a>0\) (forced population). This citing paper does not provide such a definition; instead it explicitly flags this as an open question in its concluding section: with lower bounds, “it is not clear what the definition of competitiveness should be” and asks how to treat posts that must be populated though unanimously disliked. The paper’s existing competitiveness definition uses the comparison \((x, s_x\vee 1)\) for empty posts, which relies on the possibility of leaving a post empty; this device does not directly extend to mandatory minimum occupancies. Thus the paper supplies background, motivation, and baseline results (uniqueness/efficiency of competitiveness without bounds; existence/uniqueness of fractional competitive congestion) that any extension to lower bounds would need to reconcile with feasibility constraints.
